author: [SAI] Urto666
content: why don't you do something similar to the market with rent? A player puts a "sell" rent then the other players will make "buy" offers and whoever puts the fleets in rent will choose the best bidder but still at a lower price than what he wanted to get. consequently even the fleets that don't seem convenient will be rented at profitable prices. Because it's better to rent at a lower price than to leave it there to rot!

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: You feeling sharper in the mind? That’s my favorite part. I just remembered that when I was younger I thought it would be interesting to forget my age. When I was 27 I actually did, didn’t know if I was 26, or maybe 28 off the top of my head, so I stopped myself from doing the math for a few months just to have the experience. After about a week fasting someone asked me how old I was and my mind spontaneously did the math before I could think to stop myself:)

author: 《VΛ》 KATIREcon Vantarah
content: There have been a few comments already but I did want to send a brief response to your concern. It was certainly not our intention to make players feel like they have to buy more crew. In fact, when we originally launched FICs with gen 1 recipes, we didn't get this complaint so I'm a little surprised to see it coming up now. That said, if one recipe is significantly nerfed compared to another, that's something we can try to work out. But fair warning, it will most likely involve bringing the gen 2 recipe in line with gen 1 (not the other way around). We want the recipes to be balanced but we also want to keep our emissions sustainable. We've seen a huge shift from LP/Gen1 to only Gen2 which has driven emissions up.   In short - we hear you. We apologize if it feels like this is a revenue stunt -- I assure you it's not. And, as always, we'll work to make the game balanced while maintaining sustainability.

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content: Thanks for replying. Can I raise an important issue that was possibly overlooked? (Most likely because everything done here is trailblazing, and it's impossible to predict all scenarios/exploits):  The new update looks great on paper, but backfired. Pretty badly. There's nothing now preventing a single entity from dumping 1.6m of the best recipe, and simply waiting for a week for the refresh. And repeating indefinitely.  I understand the reason behind the update, but it didn't work as intended. It hurt everyone except the very few players capable of fully exploiting the system (and also responsible for the climb up in emissions).  Can I make a (very) unpopular suggestion? 2 options:  1 - keep the update, but shrink the orders by a lot. Like, from 1.6m to 200k (random number here, just an example). This will make it not viable for that big whale to simply wait for a week, because even if he does, next week will also be only 200k added. Small orders will create what apparently you guys wanted: dynamism, and a shift towards balancing production of both recipes.  2 - get rid of the weekly refresh. Yep! Let it all rot in all 4 orders, until they're all filled. This will absolutely force those huge entities to actually move towards filling the far away bases.

content: I can't understand why the big players have an advantage over the rest? If I produce a million contracts a week - it is much more difficult for me to sell them than a player with 100 weekly contracts.  Same with the argument is that a big player can stack and wait, why can't a small one do the same? IMHO, on the contrary, in the example of the Usturs, contracts ended at the near base and I take mine to the far one, which leads me to smaller ROI than the player with smaller weekly volumes has

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content: Because of the potential impact a big player has. If you produce 1m/week and have difficulty selling it, your operation is unbalanced (as in, not enough freighting).  If a small player produces 100 contracts/week and moves it all in a single haul, it's also unbalanced (too many freighters).  The whole issue is not about the capacity to produce/move/sell. But rather about the impact each player can potentially have in the system.  If one player can wipe out an entire order in a few hours, this player has a huge advantage, indeed. Because he can do that while not being "contested" by anyone else, and simply wait until next week to do it again.  Having to move stuff around because the system is dynamic shouldn't be feared (regardless of player size). Now, being able to complete break the system in a single interaction should be prevented. It MUST be prevented.  Otherwise we're creating a hostile environment towards player acquisition/retention.  The current system is heavily skewed towards giving whales the capacity to simply ignore the entire mechanics that should bring dynamism to the system.  And guess what? It's exactly what's happening. Repeatedly. The last 3 OPOS orders in Ustur were wiped in a matter of hours.  It's not a baseless rant.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: We have some content we're pushing to help encourage people to migrate organically from Faction Fleet over to Fleet Rentals. Fleet Rentals would benefit from greater inventory availability. That content will consist of light tutorials on using the Rentals service, and some details on forming "optimal" fleets that are desirable. Otherwise, we're tracking that migration and have internal KPIs on timing around full deprecation of Faction Fleet. I agree that being too aggressive here could lead to significant sales of that inventory that is sitting in FF. And there isn't really much downside in letting it persist a bit longer given the emissions are very low relative to other game/econ systems, and they actually contribute demand to some extent in the resource (R4) economy.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content: The core purpose of the rental score is to provide a consistent, at-a-glance metric—a baseline, not a verdict. It’s not designed to be perfect, nor to substitute deep research or individual valuation. Given the diversity of fleet compositions and rental strategies—whether you're prioritizing mining yield, cargo throughput, scan range, or subwarp efficiency—no single scoring system can fairly or fully represent every user’s priorities. Trying to capture every nuanced edge case would lead to a bloated and incomprehensible model, defeating the goal of making rentals more approachable, especially for newer players.  Rather than expecting the score to be definitive, it should be seen as a useful heuristic—like a Yelp star rating for a fleet. It's someone else's snapshot judgment. And like any rating system, it should encourage people to dig deeper, not replace that investigation. Power users can (and should) use raw stats, filters, or even external tools to find undervalued rentals, while newer players benefit from a simple entry point. Ironically, the imperfections in the score create opportunity for savvy players to spot value where others don't. The score isn’t the game—it’s just a lens through which different players can start seeing it

author: mikatachan | [ROME]
content: I kind of agree with @[AEP] Lerinor here. It doesn't make sense to me to add an additional formula increasing complexity in the hope to make it easier for new people to use it.  At least for me, something more simple might be a radar / spider web chart. you have attributes which you can plot on it:  cargo size,mining rate,scanning rate,warp distance,warp fuel burn,subwarp fuel burn, Granted, some sort of scaling would need to be done, but i think this would be easier to understand than a formula new players may or may not take the time to understand / compare. you could probably do it on a scale of min/max: what's the minimum possible fleet cargo size using all ships, what's the maximum fleet cargo size using all ships.  Normalize a score like (Fleet - min) / (Max - min) x 100

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content:
@[AEP] Lerinor did a great job of analyzing current rental score system, i 100% agree with his statements, just wanted to add some visual examples. 
"The goal is to enable quick comparisons of fleet power and efficiency in the rental market." - this is what score made for, right.

Imagine i`m a new or not fully experienced SAGE player and im comparing 2 fleets of the same size x1 Armstrong IMP(7776 size score) and x1 Sledbarge(7776 size score).
What can i understand from scores on rental page?
This fleets have equal ship size (both capital),Sledbarge 2% more efficient in terms of cargo. 6723 vs 6607,Imp is 30% more efficient in terms of mining. 6297 vs 4366,
Reality:
True, fleets size is the same,Sled has 14%(6.9m more raw res every 24h) more cargo hold, imp will need 27% more fuel (2.2m) every 24h.,Imp will be able to mine 270% more copper ore every 24h (11.76m vs 4.33m), You will pay 285% more on fees+consumables mining with Sled over IMP (628 atlas vs 220 atlas every 24h).,
In this example it is obvious that we are not achieving the stated result. The player will not receive the correct data to evaluate the fleets.

And this is not only sled - imp issue,  for example capital Busan has 6592 cargo score vs 6723 on Sled, 2% difference with score, 265% difference on cargo size (608k vs 227k). 

We can discuss the form in which information should be presented to players, but in any case it should not be misleading

This fleets have equal ship size (both capital),Sledbarge 2% more efficient in terms of cargo. 6723 vs 6607,Imp is 30% more efficient in terms of mining. 6297 vs 4366, Reality: True, fleets size is the same,Sled has 14%(6.9m more raw res every 24h) more cargo hold, imp will need 27% more fuel (2.2m) every 24h.,Imp will be able to mine 270% more copper ore every 24h (11.76m vs 4.33m), You will pay 285% more on fees+consumables mining with Sled over IMP (628 atlas vs 220 atlas every 24h)., In this example it is obvious that we are not achieving the stated result. The player will not receive the correct data to evaluate the fleets.  And this is not only sled - imp issue,  for example capital Busan has 6592 cargo score vs 6723 on Sled, 2% difference with score, 265% difference on cargo size (608k vs 227k).   We can discuss the form in which information should be presented to players, but in any case it should not be misleading

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content:
@! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺  Difference in FICs receipt is part of teams vision. They dont want factions to be 100% equal on every aspect of the game, Sometimes its not fair, but i like this approach, it makes Galia more alive, real world is not equal or fair by any means.
I dont know how deep Chris balanced those receipts, but there is not only crafting time there. For example Oni fic1 and Ustur fic2 have luminate which add alot of shipping costs.
It would be correct to compare fic1 and fic2 of each faction:
Oni Fic1: 1d 18h 56min
Mud Fic1: 1d 21h 56m
Ustur Fic1: 2d 10h 43m.
As you see Ustur FIC1 need more crew, Oni - Mud are close to each other. I believe this is why Ustur has advantage on FIC2 crafting time. 
It might be worth reducing the dispersion for Ustur, I would suggest proportionally reducing crafting time of Fix1 and increasing Fix2

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: This is not so straight-forward. You can count optimal root, but most players have different fleet compositions, which is good for you - not optimal for me etc.  But the main thing is that I don't understand why we need to equalize FICs at all? It's just a mechanic of burning resources in exchange for atlas emission. It has no meaning in the long turm, LP is about building bases, it's important in the context of future release, but fix - is temporary solution to separate the big and small players.  I play on Ustur side, i care only about Ustur FICs, nothing will change for me if tomorrow MUD fics will become easier or harder to craft.

reply.preview: This is not so straight-forward. You can count optimal root, but most players have different fleet compositions, which is good for you - not optimal for me etc. But the main thing is that I don't understand why we need to equalize FICs at all? It's just a mechanic of burning resources in exchange for atlas emission. It has no meaning in the long turm, LP is about building bases, it's important in the context of future release, but fix - is temporary solution to separate the big and small players. I play on Ustur side, i care only about Ustur FICs, nothing will change for me if tomorrow MUD fics will become easier or harder to craft.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: The crafting time is the same for contracts (same for each faction). The difference in Total Production Durations appears to be due to the difference in recipes... so the TIME differential affects players who mine raw resources.  Players that buy the resources are not affected since this difference is included in the price of the contract components...affects only profits, not time.

Also, grid? I mean, I get it that we're moving towards a different coords system, but we're still at the old one. Lacking a grid (even if it's a toggle one) makes it all a bit clunky.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: I really do get where you are coming from.  I spent 6 hours the other night planning out a new FIC pivot because its easier for players with large crew counts to do FICS instead of FIC2s. I wanted to lessen some of the pressure on smaller Oni players who are competing for FIC2s. It's a harder and way more complex game loop for my fleet AND its about a 10% income loss. (FICs are objectively easier, but harder for my fleet make up.)  And I thought "Why the hell should I sacrifice my gameplay for someone else?"     And I ultimately settled on the answer that I always circle back to.  If I'm the only one left standing then I've lost everything.

author: Eoganacht | BULK [BULK]
content:
The biggest issue with Gao ships being immortal is making our target audience laugh and scroll onto their next game. We are blind to this angle now because we all stand to gain from farming mortal noobies. It is however, bad game design.

We can protect OG investments without creating a two-tiered class system of ship.

Respawn Module (Consumable Charges) Using advanced [lore bits] tech, this is a craftable item with high resource cost. There are xxs respawn modules, xs, s, etc.

Respawn Modules hold Respawn Charges (also craftable and high resource cost, in all sizes). When a vessel fitted with a Respawn Module is destroyed in any combat, the ship respawns after a timer, and 1 charge is consumed. Any ship without this module or no charges is permanently destroyed.

Accessible & Creates Economies: Respawn Module and Charge crafting is another important economic loop, with every class of ship enjoying the downstream benefits of increased economic options and specialization opportunities.

Moreover, every player new and old have access to the same opportunity for effective immortality and all the gameplay benefits therein -- at a cost.

This is a more appealing public narrative and story for Star Atlas.

When a vessel fitted with a Respawn Module is destroyed in any combat, the ship respawns after a timer, and 1 charge is consumed. Any ship without this module or no charges is permanently destroyed. Accessible & Creates Economies: Respawn Module and Charge crafting is another important economic loop, with every class of ship enjoying the downstream benefits of increased economic options and specialization opportunities. Moreover, every player new and old have access to the same opportunity for effective immortality and all the gameplay benefits therein -- at a cost. This is a more appealing public narrative and story for Star Atlas.

author: [AEP] Moustacheride
content: It seems to me more logical to make an insurance system.   for example, if you have not insured your ship, then if you lose it in Hrz, it is completely destroyed and disappears.  if you insured it, then in case of loss in Hrz, you get part of the cost in Atlas (depending on the cost of insurance) and most importantly, you get a  blueprint to which you can re-craft the lost ship (this ensures that you can regain the unique ship, even if the nft is limited and they are no longer on sale/craft)

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content:
For me the whole debate comes down to 1 question: will there be enough content in SRZ/MRZ so that players don't get bored/feel left out because they can't get into HRZ?  Web2 games are straight forward, you either play end game content or leave the project, but web3 is another beast. If you feel capped playing MRZ but not ready to risk youк assets - you have option to buy stuff from more risky players and continue your progress. One player doesn't have to be good at everything. If you are a good sage player (eco strategy) you cant be best also in shooting/ships fighting etc. This way you will have to buy stuff from UE5 fps pro gamers, you will hire crazy kamikaze guys who will be ready to risk there assets to bring HRZ recourse you need etc. 

I think we should start with total destruction in the HRZ and later, if that doesn't work, add respawn/insurance if it becomes obvious that this approach is too hardcore for the vast majority of players.

A lot of modern games have hardcore mode its obvious there is demand on it, but now people are losing time they spent on characters i agree hardcore web3 can be to much

author: [AEP] Mandalorian (Djin Djarin)
content: Many valid points have been raised. However, we should also consider that original ship owners received significant benefits, including ship parts, crew, and ample lead time to generate returns from various revenue-producing game modes. Have the ships decreased in value? Yes, as have most NFTs, to be honest. But does this mean ships should be exempt from permanent destruction? After weighing the arguments, perhaps not. Maybe there should be a level playing field with uniform insurance across all ships. Currently, newly crafted ships are at a disadvantage, starting with no components or crew. @Eoganacht | BULK @ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics

author: [AEP] Mandalorian (Djin Djarin)
content: Also another point, Yes most of us aren't game designers so its hard for us to make comments. But being all passionate gamers and seeing lots of game design issues from big game studios. Helps mold a project like this into something great. The team are also passionate gamers and can relate, but I still do believe they need to listen to their community. Not just because we helped fund it but because we are passionate as well. Of course listen to a certain extent and evaluate. But the team is good at that and they don't make rash decisions.

content: I gave it some time, and made peace with some stuff that was bothering me. Also, the new panel on the left for fleets fixed it for me. One negative point, and that persisted after giving it time: the background image. Can't use it, it pops to the front too much, and the lines create a weird false sense of unaligned grid. Using Swift's extension to go around it.  But aside from that, it's definitely been an improvement.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Many valid points have been raised. However, we should also consider that original ship owners received significant benefits, including ship parts, crew, and ample lead time to generate returns from various revenue-producing game modes. Have the ships decreased in value? Yes, as have most NFTs, to be honest. But does this mean ships should be exempt from permanent destruction? After weighing the arguments, perhaps not. Maybe there should be a level playing field with uniform insurance across all ships. Currently, newly crafted ships are at a disadvantage, starting with no components or crew. @Eoganacht | BULK @ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: People are forgetting that in Michaels original discussion there were still increased punishments to dying in HRZ.  I don't recall exactly, but it was something like 24 hour respawn, or even 7 day respawn.  So there are ways to keep the spirit of HRZ while not introducing permanent destruction.  One of Eogs argument is that people will laugh at our faces if we remove permanent ship destruction.  But I wonder what new players will think when they have to buy a $500 ship and then feel forced to play in HRZ in order to get that 10x yield.  Will they be happy with a 2x yield in MRZ when all the OGs and veterans are dominating HRZ for 10x?   I'm not so sure.  And then what happens when that noobie flies into HRZ not realizing and gets destroyed?  Thats very bad for business.   And dont say it won't happen.  Because we know it will.  Again not for or against it, just weighing the points.

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
From an economic point of view, I’m not going to comment—I don’t think I would add much value there.

On the gaming side, however, I’m advocating for a dynamic HRZ.
We live in a post-futuristic universe, so I’m sure we could find a way to contextualize it: the zone could expand or shrink based on the number of active players.

It might seem overengineered, but I’m genuinely concerned about the risk of having too few players in the HRZ. Depending on the project implementation choices, the outcome could swing from a small, overcrowded zone to a large, empty one. AI may help a lot here by acting as a substitute if few players are actually going in HRZ though.

Either way, I believe it’s going to be quite hard to balance, and I trust the team will handle it 100%.
I’ve just played a few games where the endgame wasn’t well balanced—the “real” game became reaching the endgame rather than enjoying it—and you end up with too few players in that specific stage of gameplay.

author: [COEX] 𝙈𝙪𝙖𝙙'𝙙𝙞𝙗
content: It reminds me of the old World of Warcraft days. Long ago heroic raids had just been introduced, and only those who dedicated all their energy—putting in serious time and effort—could access that content. Casual players had no real chance of reaching it, and they didn’t even mind. There was nothing to be upset about, because they knew the doors were open to them too—it was just a matter of commitment.

author: [AEP] Viktor [AEP]
content:
⁠😎┃foundation-room⁠

Hey @ROME | Winston ,
although I strongly oppose this suggestion, I appreciate your efforts  
and would therefore like to take the time to share my concerns:

If we separate GAO ships from crafted ships, we would basically lower the risks for the Medium and High-Risk-Zones as mostly GAO ships would go there. As a result, the rewards for those risk zones would have to come down by pure market forces. Think of it  like "If these rare resources aren't hard to get, their value can't be that high".

It would not provide higher and less riskier rewards to GAO-ship owners as some people might think.

I think we should rather aim for the opposite: Increase the risk (and thus the value of the rewards) for complex and non-automatable tasks as navigating and exploring medium and high risk zones with all of its dangers. 
And conversely, reduce the risk (and thus the value of the rewards) for automatable tasks like scanning or mining (currently). But that will change with the launch of C4 anyway...

I will give you an exemple how things could work : 
Let s say a GOA  ballad  worth 30 000$
a crafted ballad could cost , let s say 5000 for the example. 

price diff is 25000. 
Let s say we want this difference to be 0 after 20 dies in game , (30000-5000)/20 => 1250

If the GOA ballad is destoyed in game, it respawn in starbase but repair cost would be 5000 - 1250 => 3750$   (for now total value of this balld is 33750$)

author: Eoganacht | BULK [BULK]
content:
I personally don't think we need to price down ships. Imo this game isn't the type where every player gets the best items. 

We don't need to enable everyone to have a capital and experience that particular heroes journey. This isn't Destiny 2, this is EVE+StarCitizen+Stellaris. The experience of being in a world and even fleet/armada with these astral behemoths is a big part of the entertainment value.

The drive to build up, save, and eventually craft one of these epic ships is part of the churn of this economy.

Having every ship either insurable or fitted with respawn module/charges, protects OGs assets from total loss while providing economic burn and open access to economic opportunities for old and new players alike.

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
EVE Online is an excellent and truly hardcore game.
I spent quite a bit of time playing it back in the day, and I’d like to share a few facts.

Yes, EVE Online is a legendary universe — but far from a mainstream one. The average concurrent player count sits at around 25,000–40,000.
For comparison, casual-friendly games like WoW have hundreds of thousands online at the same time.

Most players stick to medium-risk zones, while newcomers stay in safe areas. As for null-sec (Hrz), it’s often is empty — except for a few powerful alliances camping gates chokepoints, or solo pilots flying ultra-fast or cloaked ships that are nearly impossible to catch.

Losing a ship in EVE means a real loss — and you’re only left with the insurance payout (if you had it in the first place).
But even then, unlike in Star Atlas, ships in EVE aren’t limited NFT assets — any lost ship can be rebuilt or bought again.
And even that is considered extreme hardcore, which not many players are truly willing to embrace.

It all sounds awesome on paper, but in practice, most people simply aren’t ready for that kind of risk.
That’s why balance is essential.

Losing a ship in EVE means a real loss — and you’re only left with the insurance payout (if you had it in the first place). But even then, unlike in Star Atlas, ships in EVE aren’t limited NFT assets — any lost ship can be rebuilt or bought again. And even that is considered extreme hardcore, which not many players are truly willing to embrace. It all sounds awesome on paper, but in practice, most people simply aren’t ready for that kind of risk. That’s why balance is essential.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: Not to respark this conversation, but one of the key reasons I brought it up for discussion is because the crafting economy, given current ship values, is going to lead to very long/slow construction times for larger ships. If we just use current average ROI (which is likely to change dramatically), players are earning roughly 50% ROI on game assets. That implies that losing a Commander, for example, would take someone with 100k in game assets roughly 2 years to craft one from scratch. Perhaps everyone is okay with that. But generally my concerns were not about making the game easier or eliminating permanent destruction, but thinking about potential solutions to low turnover in crafting, which also implies a perception of higher risk in HRZ. One of the reasons, imo, that EVE has been successful with hardcore PVP is that it's possible to recover (via crafting) lost ships in a reasonable amount of time. Which is why my idea centered on a separate class of ships that would be easier to craft, would lead to higher turnover, and also generate more momentum in the economy. The only way we could achieve that while also protecting GAO asset owners is to create an advantage for GAO assets, i.e. perpetual insurance/respawn. Which, btw, doesn't mean new players couldn't get access to those ships. We still have an active market for GAO ships. So anyone could still buy them on the secondary if they want that benefit. Or they can purchase the burnable ship at a lower price and face that permanent destruction risk.   I could go on about this at length, but honestly, it's not worth worrying about at the moment. And there was no shift in our philosophy. Merely a discussion. But I do think there are still economic challenges we are going to face given current ship pricing, and the hard requirement for us to not hyperinflate the economy, like everyone web2 game can do.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content:
I have to disagree with this. Crafting a ship that's worth $100k from scratch with $100k in assets should indeed take 2 years, if not more. The whole point of these ships is to be crafted collectively, adding up VWAP involved in the process.

Now, it would be an issue if I have $100k invested, and lose a $500 ship, and that one takes 2 years to craft.

The narrative that small players need incentive needs to be focused on 1 element: RNG and the possibility of finding great stuff by chance, not by using $500 in assets to 10x that in a month passively.

Otherwise, we're just creating Weimar Republic, Cyber Nation version.

Viktor gave the most accurate reasoning so far, I have to agree. Ship value is fully tied to its potential returns. Now, evaluating potential returns is the hard part. But it's absurd to think that someone owning $10k in assets should be able amass resources to craft a $100k ship in a reasonable time, by him(her)self. Unless they're very (VERY VERY) lucky in HRZ RNG stuff.

The whole point of these ships is to be crafted collectively, adding up VWAP involved in the process. Now, it would be an issue if I have $100k invested, and lose a $500 ship, and that one takes 2 years to craft. The narrative that small players need incentive needs to be focused on 1 element: RNG and the possibility of finding great stuff by chance, not by using $500 in assets to 10x that in a month passively. Otherwise, we're just creating Weimar Republic, Cyber Nation version. Viktor gave the most accurate reasoning so far, I have to agree. Ship value is fully tied to its potential returns. Now, evaluating potential returns is the hard part. But it's absurd to think that someone owning $10k in assets should be able amass resources to craft a $100k ship in a reasonable time, by him(her)self. Unless they're very (VERY VERY) lucky in HRZ RNG stuff.

author: Eoganacht | BULK [BULK]
content: There is the issue of giving too much advantage... for example, given perpetual insurance/respawn breaks the balance of power in the HRZ.  We have seen how whales affect smaller players when not limited/restricted - contracts and LP.  In no way can the advantages given to GAO ships allow whales to dominate HRZ.   I do like the idea of some advantages, but this does not mean that you are giving away HRZ to one group of whale players in the process.  Also, when you say that smaller player can buy GAO ships from the secondary market, remember that prices will appreciate in direct proportion to the advantages given. There's no guarantee that smaller players will be ablet to afford GAO ships. Actually, the opposite is most likely to occur.   I hope this explains my position a little better.  Thank you.

author: 𝐋ᴀɴᴢᴇʀ [SPS]
content: Not to respark this conversation, but one of the key reasons I brought it up for discussion is because the crafting economy, given current ship values, is going to lead to very long/slow construction times for larger ships. If we just use current average ROI (which is likely to change dramatically), players are earning roughly 50% ROI on game assets. That implies that losing a Commander, for example, would take someone with 100k in game assets roughly 2 years to craft one from scratch. Perhaps everyone is okay with that. But generally my concerns were not about making the game easier or eliminating permanent destruction, but thinking about potential solutions to low turnover in crafting, which also implies a perception of higher risk in HRZ. One of the reasons, imo, that EVE has been successful with hardcore PVP is that it's possible to recover (via crafting) lost ships in a reasonable amount of time. Which is why my idea centered on a separate class of ships that would be easier to craft, would lead to higher turnover, and also generate more momentum in the economy. The only way we could achieve that while also protecting GAO asset owners is to create an advantage for GAO assets, i.e. perpetual insurance/respawn. Which, btw, doesn't mean new players couldn't get access to those ships. We still have an active market for GAO ships. So anyone could still buy them on the secondary if they want that benefit. Or they can purchase the burnable ship at a lower price and face that permanent destruction risk.   I could go on about this at length, but honestly, it's not worth worrying about at the moment. And there was no shift in our philosophy. Merely a discussion. But I do think there are still economic challenges we are going to face given current ship pricing, and the hard requirement for us to not hyperinflate the economy, like everyone web2 game can do.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content: I know this wasn't really directed at me, but to be perfectly clear, I wasn't suggesting that it should be easy to craft large/expensive assets. Quite the opposite. It will be essential that crafting material costs and time are commensurate with the intrinsic value of the asset, and we would be using VWAP/Origination price as a benchmark for that value. Using ROI and earnings potential as a proxy to determine production times was the purpose of my comment above. Separately, what I was saying is that achieving a higher momentum and faster turnover in the economy could only be accomplished with lower value and lower cost assets, distinguished by some value mechanic.
